speaker: interviewer
text: That's where the-- that's where he needs to stand-- he needs to stand.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, wait a minute. There's an entrance where the tent is?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. That's--

speaker: interviewer
text: The washrooms. Well, washrooms-- washrooms-- they no longer exist but-- [INAUDIBLE]. Yeah, yeah, right there. [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: Listen, after 51 years, I don't know exactly [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: That's OK. We can just move there. Well, this is when [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: Put it in on.  And then I put a-- OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sigi Hart. April 19, 1996. Auschwitz, Poland. My name is Piotr Kadlcik, K-A-D-L-C-I-K. I'm conducting an interview with Sigi Hart, Auschwitz, April 19, 1996. So, please, spell your name now.

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Sigi Hart, S-I-G-I capital H-A-R-T.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, please, can you tell-- tell us about the first moment that you arrived here.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was caught in Italy in 1943, in Florence. And we were put together for transport to be deported from Italy, November 6, we were put in a train boxcar, women, men, and children. And we were transported to the train station, where we saw very long train with boxcars.

speaker: interviewer
text: And on every corner of the wagon was a sign reading 'Auschwitz.' Of course, we didn't know what Auschwitz means. I have never heard about it. And people were wondering, they were guessing, is it Germany? Is it Poland? Are they taking us to work? The Germans told us right away, we take you-- we take you to work.

speaker: interviewee
text: And men and women and children will stay together and not worry, just board the train. And you will be fed. They didn't tell us how long we will be on the road. They just gave us a little bread about this size. And we start boarding the train. And it-- when the wagon was full, we thought that this-- that this is enough people. No, the SS said, no, we have to get-- move together to get more people in the boxcart. They squeezed in about 70, 60, 70 people in a small boxcart. We had hardly room. I'm not talking about sitting, but to stand up there. They closed the doors from the boxcart. And the train started rolling. I was alone. My father escaped. My brother escaped. And I kind of joined an Italian family with a boy and a daughter and was husband and wife. And I stuck to them because I was all by myself. Like I said, the train started rolling. And we could look to a little window, where there's barbed wire. We wanted to see what direction we're going. But it was not much we could see there. Everybody wanted to go there. And we came-- we were stopped-- we were on the way about eight days. And we were stopped twice to stop to empty the buckets. We couldn't empty ourself. We had to do it in a bucket they left in the train. And once they put us food in the wagon, and then we continued. Of course, we did not-- we did not-- we did not drive straight to the-- to the camp. Because from Italy to Poland, the maximum of 12 hours in a boxcar shouldn't take eight days. But there were much more important trains. We saw trains go rolling by with-- with British prisoners and German soldiers going to the front, while we were staying side tracked. Sometimes we were standing there for all day. And we arrived in a place that's called Birkenau. Birkenau, they opened the doors. And we-- as soon as the door opened, we saw a lot of people, inmates, with stripes, clothes like pajamas. And the came to us, [NON-ENGLISH]. And they pushed us, pulled us out from the ride. We wanted to take our luggage. They said, [SPEAKING GERMAN] later, you know, you're going to get your luggage. So the women and men, everybody jumped down. And the place was here, exactly where I'm standing. We were disembarked here in this place. And there was a few SS officers with dogs. And the inmates pushed the-- the men separate and the woman with the children separate. And there was-- everybody wanted their luggage. They didn't know whether they need something to take out from the suitcases or not. But we were not allowed. We were put on the side. And a few SS officers came and started to push the people on the side. The women and children went to this side and the men on that side. They made us stay in a-- one line. And an officer went by like this, like this. And as soon as the SS officer pointed a finger, the inmate pushed and plied, pushed the men toward the women until we were about 14 people left from this transport.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people were on this transport, approximately.

speaker: interviewee
text: There were approximately 1,200. And from the 1,200, I know they-- they took 14 people. At this time, I don't know why we are separated. I tried to infiltrate myself, like, you know, to go to the women. Because I-- I rode all the time with this family and with the girl.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I tried to get to her. And one-- one prisoner, he grabbed me says, stay, stay here, don't move. So I didn't move. I didn't know that this was my lifesaver. Because if I would have been-- if it would be possible for me to go the other side, I wouldn't speaking to you now.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, we 14 people were put aside. And the women and children, there were train tracks here. They were loaded on the tracks. And an SS officer came to double check on us. He made us stay in one line again.

speaker: interviewer
text: He saw the 14 men. He said to us, is there somebody who doesn't feel good from the trip? He is tired or something? Please, let me know. I'm going to give-- we have special treatment for those people. And you have nothing to worry about. So my best friend's father, who was in the same wagon. Because my father escaped from the-- from Italy with my friend. And I got stuck with my friend's father to be deported. So my friend's father had a hernia surgery. And he says, oh, Herr Officer, I had a small surgery. As soon as the SS heard surgery, he said, oh, you go with the women and children, you get your special treatment.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he was loaded with them to the tracks. And they left. And they took us and-- to a barrack, where they told us to undress completely. We undressed. And we were walked to a second barrack. Our clothes was left there. We walked naked. It was November. We walked to the second barrack. And we had to wait outside until they call everybody in. Everybody was shaved, all their hair, underarms, on the head, and, you know, our private parts was taken off.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when I came in there, there was two or three prisoners. And they said, where are you coming from? And we told them. And they were talking in Yiddish. I know a little Yiddish. So I asked-- I told them.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we asked where the-- the rest of us were, the men and the-- the children. So there was a window. And he pointed in this direction where the woods are. There was two big chimneys. They were smoking and flames shooting out from the chimney.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he says, here, you see. This is where they coming out. And I didn't understand what he means. So I says, what do you mean they're coming out? I don't see any people. So he started kind of laughing. He says, the people come out of the chimney. They're all burned. Of course, we-- we were horrified, started crying.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they pushed us to the next barrack to take a shower. We were-- we showered. And on the end of the barrack was a man who gave us clothes, a pair of pants, a jacket, and a very-- a shirt, one shirt. We had underpants. And we had to-- to dress it. Some had too short. Some had too long. They were not-- They didn't give us by measure to suits. Whatever was available, they give us, they throw it to us, the striped clothes. And we looked at each other, you know. Few-- few-- small moment ago, we were with hair, and we were dressed in our winter coat with shoes and pants. And here we looked like monkeys with the stripes, shivering from the cold. And the men, of course, who had women and children, they cried all the time, you know. They-- they didn't know what to do. We were pulled out from the barracks and came to a place where they were few inmates sitting there. They grabbed our hands. And they tattooed a number on our hands.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was-- how are they doing this? What-- how--

speaker: interviewee
text: They had like a pen and with a needle. And they went very fast. They went like this, putting in the number. And shoved us off, you know, next. And until we all 13 had the numbers. And we waited because probably the-- the commanders didn't walk in. They-- we had to wait until everybody walks in. And then we were walked in this way here, where the quarantine camp was. The quarantine camps, I don't know why they kept the prisoners separate for four weeks. They shouldn't be sick or give any sickness to other people. Here, they were burning women and children and men. And here, the-- the opposite. They try to keep the inmates healthy from people who maybe have any sickness.

speaker: interviewer
text: So finally, we-- we arrived in a block. A block is a barrack. And, of course, all inmates. And they started calling in Polish.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I found out a little while later, they called out the numbers to distribute food. And most of the inmates here where Polish people, who talk Polish to-- to the-- to inmates. And when they called my number, I didn't understand it. So, I, of course, I didn't go forward to collect my food.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was waiting and waiting. And when everybody got their food, and I still was standing there. So I ask as I went forward to the blockalteste, who is the-- the responsible person for this barrack. I said, where's my food? So he started shouting to me and give me a kick in the butt. And-- and I ran because I don't want to get to another one. So I started to-- to ask somebody if he understand German or French, that's the language I was speaking, to teach me my number in Polish so that I don't lose any move food. Because this food was very important to survive. What kind of food it was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Food. In the evening, they gave you a three-quarter like water and there were some beets swimming around. And when you didn't eat for eight days, right, you know, then anything what you can get is food. So it was actually a soup. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So I learned my number overnight, so to say. Because the next morning when they called for the-- for the ration to-- to get food, I knew my number in Polish already. Do you remember it until now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. You want me to repeat it in Polish?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: [SPEAKING POLISH]

speaker: interviewer
text: Right? I will never forget this number because this was my name. We did not have any more names there. We got also a big number on our jackets with a triangle, a red triangle, going pointing down. The red triangle was political.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then they gave us another triangular painted in yellow, going up. What makes together the Star of David. And they put in the country from where I-- from where we were deported. Though I'm born in Germany, but I got a I for Italy.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there where a few people there who called me, oh, you Italian. Instead of my number, they called me [NON-ENGLISH], but means Italian. So I explained, I'm not Italian. I'm-- I was born in Germany. OK. This was the quarantine. The next morning, we had all to get up. What time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was around 6 o'clock. In the winter, the mornings are still dark. So they didn't want the prisoners to go out at-- in the darkness. But they-- they kept us out later in the evening to make up for it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we had to run and wash ourself. There was a little round like barrack with a few faucets on it. And everybody had to wash. And we came back here for the appel. We were standing at the appel sometimes an hour, an hour and a half, it didn't matter.

speaker: interviewee
text: Appel means a roll call. You know, when they count how many people you have. The blockalteste had to come and say he had so many people, let's say 850 or 900 people. And when they made a roll call, everything has to come out to the exact number. And if there was a number of missing, we had to stay there until he was found.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they counted us, then we had to run back in the barracks, and we got a little piece of bread not bigger than maybe two inches by four. And cup-- we didn't have a cup a day, we used our soup, we got the soup bowl. This was our-- our utilities for using for food and whatever we get. So we got a-- a-- a-- I would say, a cup of black water. I don't know if it was tea or dishwasher-- dish water or something. But it was warm. And we drank it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Some people tried to organize the-- we didn't know. We grabbed the bread. And we ate it right away. And we drink this little black juice. And this was it. But we didn't know at that time that the next food will be in the evening, the three-quarter of soup. So-- And-- and-- and this quarantine camp, we did not go out to work for obvious reasons. They put us in quarantine. We shouldn't mix with others. But they couldn't let us sit there doing nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they formed commanders. And they got separated. They got out with us to the railway station. There were boxcarts with cement and iron pipes, what has to be unloaded. And telephone poles like you see here, the big telephone poles.

speaker: interviewee
text: A cement sack was about 50 kilo, 100 pounds. And you had to take it from the-- from the boxcart. And there was a board. And you had to run down about 20 feet. Is all done in running. You have to put down. You could not throw the sack down. Because if this opened, the sack breaks the paper, it's sabotage. And this was light sabotage, you get 25 on your behind. So you get your cement sack. And you're running down this board. You're running over to the ramp. You put your sack straight. And then you run back for the next one. And if somebody was not running fast enough or he was walking, the kapo was behind him with a rubber pipe and was hitting him until he starts running. So in the evening when we came back into the camp, we had roll call. And they counted again the prisoners. And, like I said, sometimes we were an hour, sometimes two hours outside there, and it was so cold. It was November. We came from Italy. There's also winter but not like here in Poland. We were standing back to back, the-- the prisoners to warm up. And the more people we got together, we had more warmth. But then when the blockalteste came and he saw us all huddled together, he came in with a whip and get us apart, start counting again. And if everything was OK, he counted everybody, then he counted again and again, until we really got very tired, took us into the block. And they distributed the food again. This time I got my soup because I knew my number in Polish. So we were in this camp about-- oh, yeah, by the way, the next day I arrived in Auschwitz was my birthday.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you then?

speaker: interviewee
text: I just turned 17. 17? 18. I turned 18. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we went out the next day, and there was a train which falls-- with bricks to unload. And from the transport, the-- the wagon was a boxcar but open. It must have fallen snow on them. It was white. And they froze together. Now when you went to this car and you could grab a bunch of bricks, two or three, you're lucky. You could not break off bricks from it. If you're unlucky, you get eight or seven bricks, you know, you could hardly walk with them. And you-- you could not break them to-- to make lighter, you know, to take you less. And I-- I was looking for one brick or two brick. And the kapo was looking at me. And I was new. I said, well, I don't know all the rules yet. So I took one here and one there. And the kapo came, and he gave me with his fist in my-- my face. And he gave me-- showed me what I should take. And I was bleeding all over. And this was my initial-- my initiation into the quarantine lager. And this went on for one month. We were in the quarantine lager.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people were in this quarantine lager, roughly?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were about 900 people in one barrack.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: And there was at least, I would say, 20 barracks.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what countries there were from?

speaker: interviewee
text: From all the countries. They were from France, from Greece, from Italy. We were from Italy, and Dutch people and, of course, Poles. And-- [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] that's it. This was--

speaker: interviewer
text: And how you are guarded in the quarantine lager?

speaker: interviewee
text: How we were guarded? We were guarded by-- you can see the guard post here. They were standing with a machine gun. Each-- each guard had a-- a machine gun. And the guard would-- two guards with dogs were walking between the electric wires. Wherever there was a double line, they were walking with the dogs to check.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember if during this quarantine, people were trying to kill themselves? What-- what-- what was-- how the people were feeling about-- about being in such place?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was not a picnic. People were laying-- we were laying in bunks, in wooden bunks, nine in a row, when-- three high. And when you were laying first row, you-- you go in the bunk, you-- you lay down with people you don't know, and you come from the outside world, maybe the other ones came from other camps or from-- from ghettos. We came really from-- from-- from a country we were not in a camp there. It was very weird to be-- be laying down. You could not move. You can only turn around when the end decide to turn, everybody had to turn over too. You couldn't lay in the width because there wasn't enough room. And then they gave you for nine people, three or four blankets. And if you were in the middle, you always knew that you'd get a piece of blanket, that you will be warm. But when you were on the two outsides, you know you will be at night without a blanket. And then you are laying with your knees to your belly. And you're shivering because they didn't heat the-- the barracks there. So, of course, the new-- the new ones, who they called the [NON-ENGLISH], we were always on the end. And sometimes-- sometimes from the weight of the people, if the one board breaks, all nine fell on the nine below. And the nine below fall.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was a-- a whole mess. And you couldn't make much noise because if the blockalteste was listening and hear there's a-- something going on, he came out and we get all beaten. And who was the blockalteste, do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: My blockalteste happened to be a French guy, a Jewish guy, who was a-- who was a-- the blockalteste. He probably was in a different camp before. And he must've been an organizer. So he was chosen as blockalteste. He was the one who got the soup in-- in the-- in the evening. And he is the one who got all the portion of breads. They were cut. They knew from one bread, a square bread, you can make four portions. Now, they were checked out from the oberkapo too that a portion should be OK. So what they did-- what they did do, we found out later, they cut the bread. In the middle, they cut a big trench away, pushed bread together, and then they made four pieces out of it. So the blockalteste, of course, had always extra bread. And he was giving it to his friends, if there were French people from his country. So he give them the bread. And you could not exist in this camp without any help because the food was figured out for 100 to 120 calories. And in the quarantine camp, you were not working hard. And you came from the outside, so you were-- you were still fed. Not as miserable as the other people, who were called muselmann, you know, who were long in the camps and they were so skinny. And if you don't organize some food, you couldn't make it. This was after four-- after four weeks, we graduated to the working camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's stop.
